items.evidence_text:
Client: Hey. Honestly, I’m doing fine, but post-divorce I just want a clean plan—especially around taxes—so I don’t do something dumb.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: what’s your total gross income in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, gross is 153100 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a few streams, right—business, salary, and some rental income?
Client: Yeah, exactly—business income, salary, and rental income.

Client: Yeah, exactly—business income, salary, and rental income.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to the spending side for a second—what do your total monthly expenses run, roughly, for everything personal and household?
Client: It’s 4950 per month.
Advisor: Thanks. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Hey. Honestly, I’m doing fine, but post-divorce I just want a clean plan—especially around taxes—so I don’t do something dumb.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: what’s your total gross income in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, gross is 153100 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a few streams, right—business, salary, and some rental income?
Client: Yeah, exactly—business income, salary, and rental income.

Client: Correct—none. It’s 0.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—just the investable stuff first. About how much do you have across accounts you can invest—brokerage, retirement, cash, that kind of thing?
Client: Roughly five twenty-five, I mean—525050 total investable assets.
Advisor: Perfect, I’ll note that. Can you break that down for me at a high level—what buckets it’s sitting in?
Client: Sure. There’s 315450 in a taxable account on an advisor platform.

Client: And I’ve got 10650 in private markets at a brokerage.
Advisor: Understood. Any real estate we should include—your home, for example?
Client: Yeah, primary residence—value is 299950.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to be clear, you’re not carrying a mortgage or HELOC against it right now?
Client: No, nothing outstanding.

Client: Yeah, that’s exactly it—no liabilities, just want the tax side not to get sloppy.
Advisor: Makes sense, Josep. Before we wrap today, I want to confirm a couple household basics so I don’t assume wrong things.
Advisor: Marital status right now—are you single, married, divorced… what should I put down?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—any kids or anyone financially dependent on you at the moment?

Client: Hey. Honestly, I’m doing fine, but post-divorce I just want a clean plan—especially around taxes—so I don’t do something dumb.

Client: Hey. Honestly, I’m doing fine, but post-divorce I just want a clean plan—especially around taxes—so I don’t do something dumb.

Advisor: Helpful. While we’re here, I’m going to do a quick category sweep so I’m not missing anything: income we have at 153100 gross, expenses 4950 a month, assets including the taxable 315450, retirement 126700, bank cash 72200, private markets 10650, plus the home at 299950, and then protection is the disability policy—700 monthly premium, 1.12M coverage, through 2060-05-18.
Client: Yep, that all matches what I told you.
Advisor: Perfect. Next time we can dig into how you want to invest given you’re aggressive but want tax guardrails—today I mainly wanted to confirm there aren’t any hidden liabilities.
Client: Yeah, that’s exactly it—no liabilities, just want the tax side not to get sloppy.
Advisor: Makes sense, Josep. Before we wrap today, I want to confirm a couple household basics so I don’t assume wrong things.

Client: Sounds good.
Advisor: Awesome. Before we hop off, Josep, two quick admin-ish items so I set you up correctly on my side.
Advisor: First—how are you engaging with an advisor here? Is this through an RIA, a broker-dealer, your bank… what’s the channel?
Client: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Got it, thanks. And just confirming your location basics—what country are you resident in for tax and planning purposes?

Advisor: Got it. And specifically on the home—what’s the mortgage balance outstanding right now?
Client: Mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Any other debts at all—credit cards you carry, car note, personal loan—anything non-mortgage that has a balance?
Client: Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Perfect. Just to make sure I’m not mixing terms—sometimes people mean “no payment” but there’s still a balance. For you it’s actually zero balance across the board, right?

Advisor: Hi Josep—good to meet you. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client: Hey. Honestly, I’m doing fine, but post-divorce I just want a clean plan—especially around taxes—so I don’t do something dumb.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: what’s your total gross income in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, gross is 153100 a year.

Client: Other.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one: when you mentioned being in a high bracket—what bracket should we assume for planning right now?
Client: 37%.
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just so I’m not over-reading it, that 37% is your federal bracket band you want us to plan around, right?
Client: Yeah—37% federal is the right planning assumption.
